subject: reading
title: Grade 3 Reading — Middle of Year (MOY) Test 1
instructions: Read each question carefully. Choose the best answer.

passages.title: The Lost Library Book
passages.text:
Maya loved Fridays because Fridays meant a trip to the school library. Every week she checked out a new book and carried it home in her green backpack. This week she had picked a thick book about dragons, and she could not wait to read it under her favorite tree.

But on Monday morning, the book was gone. Maya searched her room. She looked under her bed, inside her closet, and even behind the curtains. The dragon book was nowhere to be found. Her stomach felt tight, like a knot that would not come undone.

"Don't worry," said her older brother Theo. "We will find it. Let's think about where you went this weekend." Maya remembered the park, her grandmother's house, and the soccer field. They had been to so many places.

Maya and Theo searched all afternoon. They checked the car, the kitchen, and the front porch. Theo even called Grandma, but she had not seen the book. Maya began to feel like she would have to tell the librarian she had lost it.

Then Theo had an idea. "What about your green backpack?" he asked. Maya had not opened it since Friday. She unzipped the front pocket, and there, tucked behind her water bottle, was the dragon book. Maya laughed out loud. The book had been with her the whole time.

That afternoon, Maya finally sat under her favorite tree and opened the dragon book. She decided that next week she would put her library books in the same place every day. A good plan, she thought, was worth more than a hundred frantic searches.

passages.title: How Bees Make Honey
passages.text:
Honeybees are small insects, but they do an important job. They help flowers grow, and they make honey that people and animals love to eat. Making honey takes many bees working together, and it begins with a single flower.

When a bee lands on a flower, it drinks a sweet liquid called nectar. The bee stores the nectar in a special part of its body called the honey stomach. This is not the same as the stomach the bee uses to digest food. One bee may visit hundreds of flowers to fill its honey stomach.

Back at the hive, the bee passes the nectar to other bees. These bees chew the nectar for a while. As they chew, the nectar slowly changes and becomes thicker. Then the bees spread the nectar inside small wax shapes called honeycomb cells.

The nectar still has too much water in it to be honey. To fix this, the bees flap their wings very fast over the honeycomb. The moving air dries out the nectar, just like wind drying wet clothes. Slowly, the watery nectar turns into thick, golden honey.

When the honey is ready, the bees seal each cell with a thin cap of wax. The sealed honey can be stored for a long time. Bees eat this honey during the winter when there are no flowers. In this way, the hive has food all year long.

The next time you taste honey, remember the hard work behind it. Thousands of bees flew from flower to flower to make that sweet treat. Without bees, we would not have honey, and many plants would not grow.

passages.title: The First Public Libraries
passages.text:
Today, almost every town in the United States has a library where anyone can borrow books for free. But this was not always true. Long ago, books were rare and expensive. Most people could not afford to buy them, and only the rich had shelves full of books at home.

In the early days, some towns started small libraries, but they were not free. People had to pay money to join, like paying for a club. Only families with extra money could become members. Poor children and workers were often left out.

A man named Benjamin Franklin had a clever idea. In 1731, he and his friends in Philadelphia pooled their money together to buy books they could share. Members paid a small fee and could borrow any book in the collection. This was one of the first lending libraries in America, but it still cost money to join.

Later, a rich businessman named Andrew Carnegie wanted to help. He believed that everyone deserved the chance to learn, no matter how much money they had. Carnegie gave money to build more than 1,600 free public libraries across the country. These buildings opened their doors to all people.

Because of leaders like Franklin and Carnegie, libraries slowly changed. Towns began to pay for libraries using taxes so that no one had to pay a fee to borrow a book. A child from any family could now walk in and pick up a book.

Libraries are more than just buildings full of books. They are places where people of all ages can read, learn, and dream. The free library we know today is the result of many people who believed that knowledge should belong to everyone.

questions.questionText: Why does Maya love Fridays?
options: She gets to play soccer.
She visits the school library.
She goes to her grandmother's house.
She reads under her favorite tree.
questions.explanation: The first sentence says Maya loved Fridays because Fridays meant a trip to the school library.

questions.questionText: What kind of book did Maya check out this week?
options: A book about soccer
A book about bees
A thick book about dragons
A book about the library
questions.explanation: The passage says she had picked a thick book about dragons.

questions.questionText: Read this sentence from the story: "Her stomach felt tight, like a knot that would not come undone." What does this sentence show about how Maya feels?
options: She feels hungry.
She feels worried.
She feels sleepy.
She feels proud.
questions.explanation: A tight stomach like a knot shows Maya is worried because her book is missing.

questions.questionText: Why had Maya not found the book in her backpack earlier?
options: She had given the backpack to Theo.
She had not opened the backpack since Friday.
The backpack was left at Grandma's house.
The backpack was too dark to see inside.
questions.explanation: The passage says Maya had not opened the green backpack since Friday, so she had not seen the book inside it.

questions.questionText: What is the main lesson Maya learns in the story?
options: Dragons are exciting to read about.
Brothers always know the answer.
Keeping things in one place helps you find them.
Libraries should not lend thick books.
questions.explanation: At the end, Maya decides to keep her library books in the same place every day, showing that a good plan beats frantic searching.

questions.questionText: Where did Maya finally find the dragon book?
options: Under her bed
Behind the curtains
In the kitchen
In the front pocket of her backpack
questions.explanation: She unzipped the front pocket of her green backpack and found the book behind her water bottle.

questions.questionText: Why does the author tell about all the places Maya and Theo searched?
options: To show how hard they looked before finding the book
To explain how the library works
To describe what the dragon book was about
To show that Maya likes to travel
questions.explanation: Listing the many places they searched shows how much effort they put in, which makes finding the book in the backpack a surprise.

questions.questionText: Which places did Maya and Theo search for the book? Select all that apply.
options: The car
The front porch
The school library
The soccer field
questions.explanation: The passage says they checked the car, the kitchen, and the front porch. They did not search the school library or the soccer field.

questions.questionText: What is this passage mostly about?
options: How bees fly with their wings
How honeybees make honey
Why people like to eat honey
Where bees build their hives
questions.explanation: The whole passage explains the steps bees take to turn nectar into honey.

questions.questionText: What does a bee drink from a flower?
options: Water
Wax
Nectar
Honey
questions.explanation: The passage says when a bee lands on a flower, it drinks a sweet liquid called nectar.

questions.questionText: Where does a bee store nectar while it visits flowers?
options: In its honey stomach
In a honeycomb cell
In its wings
In the hive
questions.explanation: The passage says the bee stores the nectar in a special part of its body called the honey stomach.

questions.questionText: In the passage, what is a honeycomb cell?
options: A tiny living creature
A small wax shape that holds nectar
A part of the bee's body
A type of flower
questions.explanation: The passage says the bees spread the nectar inside small wax shapes called honeycomb cells.

questions.questionText: Why do the bees flap their wings fast over the honeycomb?
options: To keep the hive warm
To call other bees to help
To dry out the extra water in the nectar
To seal the cells with wax
questions.explanation: The passage says the moving air from their wings dries out the nectar, just like wind drying wet clothes.

questions.questionText: The author compares the bees drying nectar to "wind drying wet clothes." Why does the author use this comparison?
options: To show that bees wash clothes
To help readers picture how moving air dries something
To explain how clothes are made
To show that honey is wet
questions.explanation: The comparison helps readers understand the new idea by linking it to something familiar—wind drying clothes.

questions.questionText: Why is it important for bees to store sealed honey?
options: So they have food in winter when there are no flowers
So they can sell it to people
So the hive looks pretty
So they can fly faster
questions.explanation: The passage says bees eat the stored honey during the winter when there are no flowers, so the hive has food all year.

questions.questionText: What happens right after a bee passes the nectar to other bees at the hive?
options: The bees seal the cells with wax.
The bees chew the nectar.
The bee drinks more nectar from a flower.
The honey is eaten in winter.
questions.explanation: The passage says the other bees chew the nectar for a while, which makes it thicker.

questions.questionText: According to the passage, what do honeybees do for nature and people? Select all that apply.
options: They help flowers grow.
They make honey to eat.
They build houses for people.
They water the plants with rain.
questions.explanation: The passage says bees help flowers grow and make honey that people and animals love to eat. They do not build houses or water plants with rain.

questions.questionText: What is the main idea of this passage?
options: Benjamin Franklin lived in Philadelphia.
Books used to be very expensive.
Free public libraries developed over time thanks to many people.
Andrew Carnegie was a rich businessman.
questions.explanation: The passage traces how libraries changed from costly clubs into free public libraries because of leaders who believed knowledge should belong to everyone.

questions.questionText: Long ago, why could most people not own many books?
options: Books were rare and expensive.
People did not like to read.
There were no schools.
Books were too heavy to carry.
questions.explanation: The passage says books were rare and expensive, and most people could not afford to buy them.

questions.questionText: What problem did the early town libraries have?
options: They had no books at all.
People had to pay money to join them.
They were only open at night.
They were too far from town.
questions.explanation: The passage explains that early libraries were not free—people had to pay money to join, like paying for a club.

questions.questionText: What did Benjamin Franklin and his friends do in 1731?
options: They built 1,600 free libraries.
They wrote a book about dragons.
They pooled their money to buy books they could share.
They asked the town to use taxes for libraries.
questions.explanation: The passage says in 1731 Franklin and his friends pooled their money together to buy books they could share.

questions.questionText: About how many free public libraries did Andrew Carnegie give money to build? Write the number from the passage.
questions.explanation: The passage says Carnegie gave money to build more than 1,600 free public libraries across the country.

questions.questionText: In the passage, what does the word "pooled" mean in "pooled their money together"?
options: Hid their money
Put their money together
Spent all their money
Counted their money
questions.explanation: "Pooled their money together" means they combined or put their money together to share the cost of books.

questions.questionText: Why did Andrew Carnegie want to build free libraries?
options: He wanted to make more money.
He believed everyone deserved a chance to learn.
He did not like clubs.
He wanted to keep books away from the rich.
questions.explanation: The passage says Carnegie believed everyone deserved the chance to learn, no matter how much money they had.

questions.questionText: How did towns finally make libraries free for everyone?
options: They paid for libraries using taxes.
They asked rich families to pay fees.
They closed the old club libraries.
They sold books to raise money.
questions.explanation: The passage says towns began to pay for libraries using taxes so that no one had to pay a fee to borrow a book.

questions.questionText: Read this sentence: "Libraries are more than just buildings full of books." Why does the author include this sentence near the end?
options: To explain how libraries are built
To show that libraries are important places to learn and dream
To prove that books are heavy
To list the rules of a library
questions.explanation: The author uses this sentence to highlight that libraries are special places where people of all ages can read, learn, and dream.

questions.questionText: Which people in the passage helped make libraries better for everyone? Select all that apply.
options: Benjamin Franklin
Andrew Carnegie
Maya
Theo
questions.explanation: The passage names Benjamin Franklin and Andrew Carnegie as leaders who helped change libraries. Maya and Theo are from a different story.

questions.questionText: What big idea does the passage share about knowledge?
options: Knowledge should belong to everyone.
Only rich people should read books.
Knowledge is not very useful.
Books should always cost money.
questions.explanation: The last sentence says the free library is the result of people who believed that knowledge should belong to everyone.
